media.text:
Your order is out for delivery 🚀

media.author: NASA

media.text:
Today, a resupply mission launched to the space station aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket bearing the @Freedom250 logo.

This mission carries several special items to celebrate this historic milestone, including semiquincentennial commemorative coins from the @USTreasury and @usmint, along with multiple sets of new @USPS stamps, and our own NASA Freedom 250 patches.

For more information about our celebration of America’s 250th year, visit nasa.gov/freedom250

media.text:
"It’s great to be with you in the people’s house."

This week, NASA’s Artemis II crew visited the U.S. Capitol to speak with lawmakers, congressional staff, and special guests about their historic 10-day mission around the Moon and back.

And, yes, Rise was there too!

media.text:
To build on the Moon, we need construction materials that can withstand bitter cold – so @NASAglenn invented a machine that can recreate that environment for testing!

This high-powered refrigerator, or cryocooler, removes heat without using any liquid. go.nasa.gov/3PGqIR9
media.author: NASA

media.text: Teams stood down from today’s launch opportunity due to inclement weather. We're now targeting no earlier than 6:05 p.m. EDT Friday, May 15, for the launch. Details: go.nasa.gov/4uPQe5x

media.text: Planning is underway for our Artemis III mission, which will test the capabilities of NASA's Orion spacecraft and one or both commercial landers from @BlueOrigin and @SpaceX as they meet and dock in Earth's orbit. Details: go.nasa.gov/4nvfifB

media.text: LIVE: Experts talk about the next cargo launch to the @Space_Station. This delivery will include supplies and science experiments, including one that will evaluate how red blood cells and the spleen change in space to protect future astronauts. piped.video/live/-SejIZD6b8k

media.text:

media.text: The next delivery of NASA science and equipment to the @Space_Station is set for no earlier than May 12. In addition to experiments, these resupply missions also bring supplies that allow the crew aboard to continue living and working in low Earth orbit: go.nasa.gov/3Qe8wyp

With Artemis II safely back on Earth, NASA has begun shipping the core stage for the Artemis III rocket to @NASAKennedy.

media.text:
One week has passed since Artemis II returned to Earth after a historic mission around the Moon. We laughed, we cried, and we pushed the boundaries of what’s possible, setting the stage for future innovation and exploration.

We’re just getting started.
media.author: NASA

media.text: "Victor, Christina, Jeremy, just... thank you. This was an unbelievable adventure and it was made possible by this crew and the support of each other throughout the whole thing." - @Astro_Reid, commander of Artemis II.
media.author: NASA

media.text: “The difference now is, when we come before you now, we’ve done this together. We took your hearts with us, and your hearts lifted our hearts.” - @Astro_Christina on the positive impact of the mission.
media.author: NASA

media.text: "We did what we said we were going to do. And now we've got to step out and just face that reality—that's a great thing." - @AstroVicGlover on the public's interest in the mission.

Now that the Artemis II crew is home, what's next for our Artemis program?

We're preparing to launch Artemis missions every year. Artemis III is next up in 2027, with Artemis IV landing on the Moon in 2028.

Learn more about the Artemis mission cadence: go.nasa.gov/4voiflP

media.author: NASA

media.text:
HOME.
 
The Artemis II crew has arrived back on Earth, ending a nearly 10-day journey around the Moon. The trip took them farther into space than humans have ever gone before, and now they're safely home with us.

go.nasa.gov/41r9eL0
media.author: NASA

media.text: Big smiles from Christina and Victor on the deck of the USS John P. Murtha, as they waited to be escorted for their routine post-mission medical checks.
media.author: NASA

media.text: All four of the Artemis II astronauts have been successfully extracted from the Orion spacecraft following splashdown and are now on the USS John P. Murtha. Next up, they will be escorted to the medical bay where they will undergo post-mission medical evaluations.
media.author: NASA

media.text:
Welcome home Reid, Victor, Christina, and Jeremy! 🫶

The Artemis II astronauts have splashed down at 8:07pm ET (0007 UTC April 11), bringing their historic 10-day mission around the Moon to an end.
media.author: NASA

media.text:
Orion's main parachute has deployed. The spacecraft has a system of 11 chutes that will slow it down from around 300 mph to 20 mph for splashdown.

Get more updates on the Artemis II blog: nasa.gov/blogs/artemis/
media.author: NASA

media.text: The crew module on Orion has separated from its service module. After traveling around the Moon, seeing its far side, and experiencing a solar eclipse, the Artemis II astronauts are on the last leg of their trip home.
media.author: NASA

media.text:
After a journey of more than 690,000 miles, the crew is nearly home.

The Artemis II crew will splash down off the coast of San Diego later today and, though it won’t be visible from land, you can still wave in their general direction to welcome them back to Earth! 👋
media.author: NASA

media.text:
Your close personal friends, the @NASAArtemis astronauts (and Rise), can't wait to get home!

In the meantime, enjoy a few new pictures from their camera roll. And watch their return with us live at nasa.gov/live.

media.text:
Artemis II astronauts have traveled 252,756 miles from Earth, flown around the Moon, and observed the lunar surface like never before. Now, they’re coming home. 🌎 

Watch the crew splash down on Friday, April 10, around 8:07pm ET (0007 UTC April 11). nasa.gov/ways-to-watch/

media.text:
Wake up—it's Artemis II's last day in space!

media.text:
Homeward bound 🌎

items.text: This is the unsexy hero tech right here. Everyone talks rockets, but if your bolts, concrete, or wiring can’t survive lunar night temps, you’re toast. What temps can that cryocooler hit?

items.name: NASA
items.text: The Lunar Environment Structural Test Rig – LESTR for short – can test materials, electronics, and other flight hardware at temperatures as low as 40 Kelvin, or about –388 degrees Fahrenheit.

items.name: Facts Unhinged
items.text: The Moon’s surface drops to ❄️ minus 388°F at night. Rubber shatters like glass. Circuit boards fail. Electrical connections freeze and crack. Testing materials before sending them there isn’t optional it’s the whole game. This rig is genuinely brilliant engineering. Follow @FactsUnhinged for daily mind blowing facts 🤯

items.name: Samuel Tinnerholm
items.text: Liquid-free cryocoolers are huge for sustained lunar ops. Permanently shadowed regions hover near 40 K and you can't keep shipping LN2 or LHe to the Moon. Closed-cycle Stirling / pulse-tube systems are exactly the boring, critical infrastructure that lets a base actually stay long-term.

items.text: I think what NASA is doing here is exactly the kind of problem-solving that changes industries. Most people overlook infrastructure — they want the rockets and the moon bases. But none of that works without solving the boring stuff first, like materials that won't fail at -180°C. In business, it's the same thing. The unglamorous foundation work is what keeps everything standing when conditions get brutal.

NGC 6334 is one of the most active star-forming regions in our galaxy — roughly 5,500 light-years away in Scorpius. The magenta you're seeing is X-ray light from Chandra, revealing the hottest, youngest stars hiding inside those dust clouds that Webb's infrared can penetrate but visible light can't.
Two observatories. One complete picture. 🐾
#Astronomy #NebulaSeason #JamesWebb #ChandraXray #SpaceScience

🌟 The real detail that gets me  on a circumbinary planet orbiting two stars, you would cast two shadows simultaneously.
As the stars move relative to each other across the sky, your shadows would slowly converge, cross, and separate again throughout the day.
Kepler 16b was the first confirmed circumbinary planet, found in 2011. Surface temperature around -100°C. Not habitable.
But 24 more candidates just confirmed by TESS means the question is no longer whether these planets exist. It's how many there are and whether any of them could be.
George Lucas imagined a double sunset in 1977. Took astronomers 34 years to find the first real one.
📌 Source: NASA TESS circumbinary planet survey, May 2026

English version:

Gray goo was once a widely discussed concept.

What AI is now warning about is the self-replication of biomachines, specifically artificial immune cells.

Viruses already possess the ability to replicate themselves. If artificial immune cells were to go out of control, they could consume Earth’s resources and continue to multiply.

Viruses can adapt to their environment, producing dozens of mutated variants over time. As they consume ocean resources, the color of the seas would also change.

A green-colored planet may indicate a phase of viral proliferation.

Do not approach it under any circumstances.

items.name: Chronos Intelligence
items.text:
🔴 Curiosity landed in 2012. Designed for a 2 year primary mission.
It is now in year 14. It has driven 33 kilometers across a planet with no roads, no mechanics, and no spare parts. It drills rock, runs chemical analysis, and transmits data across 225 million kilometers of space every single day.
When it landed, the iPhone 5 had just been announced. That phone is now obsolete, unsupported, and in a landfill somewhere.
Curiosity is still working.
Six years of this timelapse is one chapter. The rover has been accumulating dust, climbing mountains, and finding ancient lake beds since before most of its current mission scientists finished their PhDs.
No human has ever seen Mars with their own eyes. Everything we know about that surface came from machines we trusted to go alone.
📌 Source: NASA Curiosity mission logs, JPL Mars Science Laboratory 2012-2026

When you consider what these six years mean for the equipment and the rover, and realize that everything has to run smoothly out there—even the slightest mistake could mean the loss of the rover—you get a sense of just how demanding space exploration is.

But our curiosity drives us to go further and further.
To continue exploring. And in the future, rovers won’t just be on Mars, but also on distant worlds like Pluto or Sedna.

items.name: Armaan Sidhu
items.text:
NASA added Jordan as the 63rd signatory of the Artemis Accords.

Everyone reads this as soft diplomacy. It's colonial governance paperwork for the Moon.

The Accords are the US-led framework for civil space exploration. Principle five affirms space mining is "lawful under the Outer Space Treaty." Principle 11 lets signatories establish "safety zones," exclusion perimeters the US and its allies get to draw first.

Outer Space Treaty of 1967 forbids sovereignty claims in space. The Accords built the legal workaround.

China and Russia run the International Lunar Research Station, the parallel bloc. Pakistan, Egypt, South Africa, Venezuela, Belarus signed on. The Moon's south pole is the prize. Whoever parks hardware next to the water ice first declares a safety zone no one else can enter.

This is how empires get built. Not by claiming territory. By defining rules before anyone shows up, then having the hardware to enforce them.

63 signatories is the majority. The US built a coalition large enough to define "international consensus" before China's ILRS hit 20. By the time taikonauts land in 2030, the legal architecture already favors whoever parks a lander near Shackleton Crater first.

Jordan brings zero rockets. Jordan brings a vote.

Water ice means hydrogen. Hydrogen means rocket fuel. Rocket fuel on the Moon means Mars access controlled by whoever pumps it.

The scramble for Africa had a rulebook. The Berlin Conference of 1884.

The scramble for the Moon has one too. NASA headquarters, 2026.

items.name: Uzay ve Bilim
items.text:
This shimmering region of star-formation, a close-up of the Trifid Nebula about 5,000 light-years from Earth, was captured in intricate detail by NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope. The colors in Hubble’s visible light image, which marks the 36th anniversary of the mission's launch on April 24, are reminiscent of an underwater scene filled with fine-grained sediments fluttering through the ocean’s depths.

Several massive stars, which are outside this field of view, have shaped this region for at least 300,000 years. (See them in a wider view.) Their powerful winds continue to blow an enormous bubble, a small portion of which is shown here, that pushes and compresses the cloud’s gas and dust, triggering new waves of star formation.

items.name: Chronos Intelligence
items.text:
🌑 The last time humans landed on the Moon was December 1972.
Gene Cernan stepped off the lunar surface and said he believed humanity would return soon. Then he climbed back into the lander.
Nobody went back. 52 years passed.
An entire human lifetime could begin and end between Apollo 17 and the moment Artemis IV puts someone on the surface in 2028.
Every person currently alive under the age of 56 has never lived in a world where humans walked on the Moon. That is the majority of the planet.
Artemis IV will not be a return for most people watching. It will be the first time in their entire lives.
Same Moon. Completely different world looking up at it.

items.name: Chronos Intelligence
items.text:
🌑 Ten days ago these four people were sitting on a launchpad in Florida.
They then traveled 690,000 miles, flew around the Moon at 60,000 miles per hour, watched Earth rise from the lunar surface, broke a 56 year old human distance record, survived 40 minutes of total communication blackout behind the Moon, and hit the Pacific Ocean inside a fireball moving at 25,000 miles per hour.
They are now sitting on a Navy ship eating and the world has already moved on to the next thing.
The Apollo astronauts got ticker tape parades through cities. The entire planet stopped.
Same species. Same Moon. Completely different attention span!

items.text:
Delivering over 11,000 pounds of research and supplies to the ISS via a Cygnus spacecraft CRS-24 key cargo includes studies on stem cell production, quantum physics (Cold Atom Lab), and human microbiome health to aid future Artemis missions. 

NASA astronauts Jack Hathaway and Chris Williams will be on duty during the Cygnus spacecraft’s approach and rendezvous. Hathaway will be at the controls of the Canadarm2 robotic arm ready to capture Cygnus as Williams monitors the spacecraft’s arrival.

Credit: @NASA

NASA's Northrop Grumman CRS-24 (NG-24) resupply mission is launching Saturday, April 11, 2026, at 7:41 a.m. EDT.  

A SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket from Cape Canaveral Space Force Station (SLC-40) will carry the Cygnus XL spacecraft — named S.S. Steven R. Nagel after NASA astronaut Steven Nagel — with ~11,000 pounds of science experiments, supplies, and equipment to the International Space Station.  

This is the second flight of the larger Cygnus XL version. It will support ongoing research and crew operations aboard the ISS. Docking is planned for April 13.  

Watch live coverage: nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-s…

items.name: Chronos Intelligence
items.text:
🌑 In 13 minutes Orion will hit Earth's atmosphere at 23,864 miles per hour.
For 6 minutes the capsule will be completely unreachable. Plasma wraps the hull and no signal gets in or out. Mission Control just waits.
The last time humans came home from this far away was Apollo 13 in 1970. That crew nearly didn't make it back at all.
This crew went 4,111 miles farther. Flew behind the Moon with no contact with Earth for 40 minutes. Ate maple cookies in the dark.
Now 13 minutes stands between 252,756 miles of space and the Pacific Ocean.
Apollo 13 made it home on a lifeboat with 15 minutes of power left. Artemis II comes home named Integrity.
Same ocean. Very different journey getting there.
